title: Giant Prehistoric Insects: Why They Were So Big Is Still a Mystery!
summary:
Imagine a dragonfly with wings as wide as a seagull! 300 million years ago, insects like that really flew around Earth. For a long time, scientists thought they got so big because the air had more oxygen. But a new study says that might not be the whole story.

Insects don't have lungs. They breathe through tiny tubes called tracheoles (TRAY-kee-ohls). Scientists believed bigger insects needed more oxygen, and high oxygen levels let them grow huge. But a team led by Edward Snelling from the University of Pretoria used powerful microscopes to check insect flight muscles. They found that tracheoles take up only about 1% of the muscle—even in giant prehistoric insects. That's tiny! So oxygen probably wasn't the limit.

"If oxygen really set the limit, we'd see more tracheoles in bigger insects," said Snelling. "But there's hardly any difference."

So what made those insects so big? Maybe they needed to escape predators, or their bodies just couldn't get any bigger. Scientists aren't sure yet. It's a cool mystery waiting to be solved!
why_it_matters: This shows that science always learns new things, and even old ideas can change with new evidence.

keywords.explanation: From a time before people wrote history.
An insect with long wings that lives near water.
Tiny tubes in insects that help them breathe.
Tools that make tiny things look big.
Animals that hunt other animals for food.

questions.question: What did scientists study to test the oxygen idea?
How much of the flight muscle was tracheole space in giant insects?
What did people think made giant insects so big?

background_read: The Carboniferous Period (about 300 million years ago) had giant insects like Meganeura, a dragonfly with a wingspan of 2.5 feet.
Scientists once thought high oxygen levels allowed those insects to grow huge, but this new study by Edward Snelling (a scientist at the University of Pretoria) questions that idea.
Using powerful microscopes, they looked at fossil insect muscles and found that breathing tubes took up very little space.
Article_Structure: WHO: Edward Snelling and his team at the University of Pretoria.
WHAT: They studied insect flight muscles and found tracheoles took only 1% of the muscle, so high oxygen might not explain giant size.
WHERE: At the University of Pretoria in South Africa.
WHY: To understand why giant prehistoric insects were so big.

perspectives.description: They are excited about the new discovery and want to solve the mystery.
They can learn that science is full of surprises and asking questions is important.
They find it interesting to learn about prehistoric life and how it changed over time.
